turns.text: I asked a question of the prosecutor. What restraints did the-SA man not have in contrast to others? That is how I under-stood the question.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Was the SA man as free in his behavior, or was the SA man more free in his behavior than the ordinary German citizen? If you cannot answer it, have a look for a moment at the general service regulations which you talked about just now. My Lord, that is on Page 30-A of Document Book B. It is Docu-ment Number 2820-PS, and is Exhibit USA-427. [Turning to the witness.] Look first at Article I. I think it is on Page 9. Have you got it?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: "The SA man is the political soldier of Adolf Hitler"; and a few lines further down: "He therefore enjoys special prestige and has definite rights in the State." Do you deny that those words mean what they say? Wasn't the SA man in a privileged position?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: I can only say that as far as I was an SA 'man, and as far as I came to know others, SA men were not in a privileged position. Besides, this is the SA service regulation of 1933, which, according to my knowledge, was rescinded essentially in 1934, and ...

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: I do not care when it was rescinded. It was issued on the 12th of December 1933, was it not? And that was after the Nazis were in power? [The witness made no response.]

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Well, you can see it says so on the top of it. Tell me what those definite rights in the State were that the SA man is said to have by Article I. What were the definite rights in the State? What did it mean? Every SA man read that book.

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: If the SA man was in the service of the State or in the emergency police service, he, of course, had the privileges accorded that particular service.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: You cannot tell me what they are, I suppose. Well, look at that Article 10 on Page 13. Have you got Article 10, Page 13?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: Ten? Yes.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: "The exalted position of the SA man may not be degraded by insulting, slighting, or unjust treatment." How was the SA man "exalted" above any other German ,citizens?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: In my opinion he only had particular responsibilities.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: What did it mean when it says he had "an exalted position," and he must not be insulted? He could insult other German citizens, could he not? [The witness made no response.]

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Was the SA man e3falted above the Army? Yes or no?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: I have already said that, as far as I am concerned, I never had or assumed any special privilege, and therefore I cannot imagine that the SA man could have availed himself of any such privilege.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Very well then; that is your answer. Now, look at Article 18, on Page 17: "The SA man may use weapons which are entrusted to him only in execution of his service or for legal self-protection." Now I want you to tell me, what aspect of the SA man's service might require the use of weapons other than in self-defense?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: I have already said that the SA man could be used for emergency service. With regard to these service regulations, I would like to say that in my opinion they had been issued under Röhm at the time ...

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: I do not want to go into that. R6hm was Chief of Staff of the SA, and what he issued presumably was law to the SA. And he says that they may use weapons only in execution of their service or for legal self-protection. Now I ask you again, apart from self-protection, what case could there be where the SA man's service should require the use of a weapon? If you cannot answer, say so.

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: I can only say what I have already said in answering a question of counsel, that the SA was armed only to the extent that it was active in carrying out functions of the State.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Are you suggesting that the purpose to which they might use their arms might be a military purpose, then? [The witness made no response.]

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Are you suggesting they might use them for a military purpose, if they were called for that purpose?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: I have said emergency service, especially auxiliary police service or police service, whenever the SA was called upon to do so.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: You say you are not asserting they would use them in the Army, but you are asserting they might use them to assist the Police, are you?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: For police emergency service, or police auxiliary service.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Do you mean, then, that when they were under the police auxiliaries, this regulation in the general service regulations of the SA was the regulation that applied to them? Or did police regulations apply? [The witness made no response.]

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Did they take the commands of the SA, or did they take the commands of the Police when they were auxiliary policemen? That is what I want to know.

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: Mr. Prosecutor, I have only stated what I have seen myself. I do not know what has been decreed in detail according to the service regulations. The SA man, as I have seen it, was armed in as far as he was used in the State or police auxiliary service.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Can you tell me any other case besides police service where he would have to use his arms, except self-defense? Any other case? [The witness made no response.]

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: I put it to you, Witness, that what these arms which are mentioned in this Article 18 were meant for was nothing more nor less than for the carrying out of the so-called SA actions; isn't that right?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: I can, only repeat again and again that in my opinion ...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Witness, you can answer the question. It is either right or it is wrong. You can say, you were with the SA all this time.

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: If the SA man used the weapons when not employed on emergency service, then he became liable to punishment. Apart from that, the SA man was used only for emergency service.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: The SA man, I put it to you, became liable for punishment if he used his weapons for a purpose that the SA did not approve of. But what I am saying now is that he was encouraged-indeed, ordered-to use his weapons for actions which the SA did approve of. [The witness made no response.]

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Well, if you cannot answer that, I will leave it. Look on in that little book to one more thing. Look on to Page 33, Number 6 of the punishment regulations; Page 33. Have you got Page 33?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: Yes, I have Page 33.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Now, you see the last sentence of the first paragraph, about punishment: "Right is what is advantageous to the Movement, and wrong is what harms it." Have you got that?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: "Right is what is advantageous to the Movement, and wrong is what harms it."

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: Yes, I have found that.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Now, I suggest to you, Witness, that what is advantageous to the Movement, such as SA actions, is precisely the thing that the SA arms and weapons were meant to be used for; is that right or wrong? You can say yes or no.

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: The SA leaders were employed under the command of their leaders, and they had to know for what purposes they were allowed to employ their SA men.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: I do not think that has got much to do with my question. Look again at that sentence, "Right is what is advantageous to the Movement, and wrong is what harms it." Does not that show perfectly clearly that the Nazi Party regarded the SA as a privileged party who were entitled to commit crimes if they were advantageous to the Movement?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: The SA man was led, and could not on the basis of that regulation act as an individual, or as he wanted to.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: My Lord, I have only got one more document. There are only two or three questions on it, My Lord.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: My Lord, the document is the first document in Book C. It is D-918. Oh, I beg Your Lordship's pardon. It is Book 16-B. The document is D-918 and it will become Exhibit GB-594. Witness, I am not going to take you into any detail in this docu-ment. You can see what it is. It is Lutze's training directive for 1939, and you will see on Page 2 that the date on which it was issued was 4 November 1938, which was before Hitler's order about the pre- and postmilitary training. Now I have only one point to, put to you on this document. You have maintained just now, have you not, that the training of the SA was predominantly for sport; is that right?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: I have said that the training of the SA was primarily training and exercise towards the achievement of the Sport Insignia and ideological and physical training generally.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: But didn't you say that the emphasis was placed upon sport and not upon military tendencies? If you didn't say that, admit it.

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: I cannot remember the details of what was said before.  I can only say one thing, that the SA only had defense-sports training, including physical and ' intellectual training and training of the will power as described here in this book.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: You don't deny then that that training had a military tendency behind it; do you deny that? The training for the Sport Insignia had a military tendency behind it?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: We received no directives for any kind of military train-ing nor did we actually engage in it. It was a case of moral education, comprising, as I should like to point out again and again, physical and intellectual training and training of the will-power, and nothing else.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Well, all I want you to do is to run your eye down certain passages of this document. Look at Page 7 of Lutze's training directive for 1939. You will see that Page 7 deals with the first training period, from November 1938 until the beginning of February 1939, and at the bottom part of the page you will see, set out in certain sequence, the items on which particular attention is laid: Marching, drill, shooting, field training, and last of all, sport. Can you see that?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Now turn on to Page 9, which gives you a similar thing for the second training period, from February to April 1939. In the middle of the page you will see, underlined again: Drill, firing training, and last of al.1, sport. Do you see that?

turns.speaker: BOCK
turns.text: I do not know, Mr. Prosecutor, what you are referring to right now-I have it now.

turns.speaker: MAJOR BARRINGTON
turns.text: Now, turn on to Page 10, where you see the same thing for the third and last training period, which is May to June 1939. On Page 10 you will see the same thing: drill, musketry, field training, and last of all, sport. Isn't it perfectly clear that sport was very much an excuse and a means to an end? My Lord, I am not proposing to put any more questions to this witness, as the general topics will be dealt with in the cross-examination of the witness Juettner.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well; we will adjourn now. [The Tribunal adjourned until 13 August 1946 at 1000 hours.]

preamble: [The witness Von Manstein resumed the stand.]
